scenario_title: Add a record at a name that already owns CNAME
test_rationale: This test starts with a minimal zone containing a single CNAME owner and then attempts one unsigned UPDATE that adds an A RR at that same owner name. The prerequisites are intentionally absent so the only decision point is how the server resolves the CNAME-versus-other-data conflict. RFC 2136 does not prescribe a specific refusal code or overwrite behavior for this case, while RFC 2181 only gives the coexistence rule as a guideline, making this a good binary-oracle candidate based on both the UPDATE reply and the post-update QUERY results.

group_1.behavior: Reject the UPDATE when adding a non-CNAME RR to a name that already owns a CNAME, typically preserving the original CNAME-only state.
group_1.post_state: QUERY ANY returns only the original CNAME; QUERY A returns no A RR.

group_2.behavior: Accept the UPDATE and retain or create coexisting data at the owner name despite the pre-existing CNAME, or otherwise handle the conflict without the same refusal code.
group_2.post_state: QUERY ANY or QUERY A shows the added A RR, possibly alongside the original CNAME.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2136 defines the mechanics of add_rr but does not specify a special update-state transition for inserting a non-CNAME RR at an owner name that already has a CNAME. RFC 2181 §10.1 says a CNAME should not coexist with other data, but it does not normatively pin the dynamic-update RCODE or mandate whether the server must refuse, replace, or reorder the change. That leaves a protocol gap that can surface both in the UPDATE reply and in the resulting RRset state.
